text:
This article examines the specialized terminological framework employed by Muhammad Said Ramadan al-Buti in his seminal work, *al-Salafiyyah*. It offers a detailed analysis of the "Arabian scales," the "scientific and ideological shield," and the "compass"—concepts that the Sheikh identifies as the essential pillars of the *manhaj* (methodological path) established by the righteous predecessors (*salaf*). These pillars are rooted in a combination of sincere faith and the linguistic precision of pristine Arabic speech.

By systematizing evidence from across the book, the research characterizes the scholars who have remained faithful to these "scales of sanity" versus those who have deviated from them, outlining the theological consequences of each path. Furthermore, the paper investigates al-Buti’s stance on the permissible boundaries of inquiry. It explores his nuanced response to the strict prohibition of questioning favored by extreme conservatives, as well as his critique of the unrestrained authorial judgment advocated by liberal reformists. Through an examination of al-Buti’s classification of knowledge and his refined linguistic style, the author demonstrates how this methodology successfully navigates between these ideological extremes, ultimately articulating a balanced and moderate framework for contemporary Islamic theology.
